title: Variables and expressions
boardSubtitle: Aim with fixed inputs first, then use an assignment to store and reuse the portal side.
unlockSpotlightText:
A variable lets you store a value under a name.

New game action: before dropping the ball, write 'choose_input(...)' to choose which input the ball launches from.

unlockPrimerCards.title: What a variable is
unlockPrimerCards.body: A variable is a named place in the program where you can store a value and use it again later.

unlockPrimerCards.title: What assignment does
unlockPrimerCards.body: 'variable_name = 3' is an assignment statement. It stores the value 3 in a variable called 'variable_name'.

unlockPrimerCards.title: Use the stored value
unlockPrimerCards.body: 'choose_input(3)' launches from the right input. If 'variable_name' stores 3, 'choose_input(variable_name)' does the same thing.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 1: Drop a ball from input 1 (left)
practiceGoals.instruction:
Change the main program.
Add the line 'choose_input(1)' before 'drop_ball()' and run the program.
practiceGoals.boardHint:
Put 'choose_input(1)' before 'drop_ball()'. The first line selects the left input, and the second line launches the ball.

If 'choose_input(1)' comes after 'drop_ball()', it is too late for this ball.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 2: Drop a ball from input 3 (right)
practiceGoals.boardHint:
Keep the two-line shape, but change the number inside 'choose_input(...)' to 3.

The inputs are 1 = left, 2 = center, 3 = right.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 3: Drop the ball toward the portal
practiceGoals.instruction:
A portal is on the board. Balls that reach it split for extra value.

The Board data panel shows 'portal_side': the input number under the portal.

Use 'portal_side' in 'choose_input(...)' and launch one ball toward the portal. You may use it directly or store it first with 'target = portal_side'.
practiceGoals.boardHint:
'portal_side' is already the portal input number. You can write 'choose_input(portal_side)' directly, or first store it as 'target = portal_side' and then write 'choose_input(target)'.

Good code can read the board instead of relying on fixed inputs.

primerCards.title: What the board gives you
primerCards.body: The board shows you which side the portal is on. Python reads that same side as 'portal_side'.

title: Conditionals
boardSubtitle: Read 'next_ball' before the launch and choose the right response.
unlockSpotlightText:
Special balls are now in the queue.

🔴 Negative ball (negative_ball): costs points if launched
🟢 Center ball (center_ball): earns a bonus from input 2
🔵 Portal ball (portal_ball): splits harder in the portal

Board data shows the next ball as 'next_ball'. Use 'if', 'elif', and 'else' to route each type, and use 'skip_ball()' to discard negative balls.

unlockPrimerCards.title: What 'if', 'elif', and 'else' do
unlockPrimerCards.body: 'if' checks the first condition. 'elif' lets you check another condition if the first one was false, and 'else' handles everything left over.

unlockPrimerCards.title: What the board gives you
unlockPrimerCards.body: The board shows the next ball before the launch. The same value is stored in the variable 'next_ball' in code.

unlockPrimerCards.title: Your routing plan
unlockPrimerCards.body:
1. Skip 'negative_ball'
2. Send 'portal_ball' to the portal
3. Send 'center_ball' to input 2

practiceGoals.title: Goal 1: Skip 'negative_ball'
practiceGoals.instruction:
The next ball is 'negative_ball'.
Write an 'if' statement that checks 'next_ball == negative_ball' and skips it with 'skip_ball()'.
Run the program, but launch no ball. The launch branch comes in the next goal.
practiceGoals.boardHint:
Smallest working shape:
if next_ball == negative_ball:
    skip_ball()

'skip_ball()' removes this bad ball from the queue. The run should happen, but this goal should launch no balls.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 2: Send the remaining balls toward the portal
practiceGoals.instruction:
Keep the rule for skipping the negative ball.
Add another branch for the safe balls. You can use 'else', or you can already start using 'elif' and 'else'.
Launch the remaining balls from the input above the portal.
practiceGoals.boardHint:
Keep the first branch for the negative ball. Add an 'else:' below it, which runs when the ball is not negative.

Inside the 'else' branch, choose 'portal_side' and launch the ball.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 3: Launch 'center_ball' from input 2
practiceGoals.instruction:
In this situation the next ball is 'center_ball'.
Delete the current code and launch it from input 2.
practiceGoals.boardHint:
The next ball is definitely 'center_ball', so a direct center launch is enough.

Choose input 2 with 'choose_input(2)', then launch.

title: Functions
boardSubtitle: Store one reusable tactic in the helper editor and call it from the main program.
unlockSpotlightText:
The helper editor is unlocked.
Store one helper function there, then call it from the main program whenever you need the same tactic again.

unlockPrimerCards.title: What a function is
unlockPrimerCards.body: A function is a named block of code that you can run again later.

unlockPrimerCards.title: Helper editor role
unlockPrimerCards.body: Keep one helper function in the helper editor. Run it from the main program with one command.

unlockPrimerCards.title: Portal tactic
unlockPrimerCards.body: Write a helper that launches from the input above the portal.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 1: Call the helper
practiceGoals.instruction:
Call 'follow_portal()' once.
The helper reads 'portal_side' from the board, so you do not choose the side yourself.
practiceGoals.boardHint:
The helper editor already defines 'follow_portal()'.

In the main program, call it with one line: 'follow_portal()'.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 2: Reuse the helper
practiceGoals.instruction:
Use the same helper again.
The portal is on the other side this time, but the helper still reads 'portal_side' dynamically, so the same call should still work.
practiceGoals.boardHint: A helper that reads 'portal_side' at run time still works when the portal moves. The main program can stay as 'follow_portal()'.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 3: Write 'skip_negative()'
practiceGoals.instruction:
Edit the helper function.
Rename it to 'skip_negative()'.
It should skip the visible 'negative_ball' and launch nothing.
Call it from the main program with the new name.
practiceGoals.boardHint:
Edit the helper editor. The function name must be 'skip_negative' because the main program calls 'skip_negative()'.

Inside that function, use 'skip_ball()' only.

title: Loops
boardSubtitle: Use a loop to automate a short preview response.
unlockSpotlightText:
A loop repeats the code inside it several times. That means one loop can handle and launch multiple balls in a single run.
Inside that loop you can also use 'if', 'elif', and 'else' to react to different balls.
If you skip a ball, it is smart to use 'continue'. That stops the current loop step and starts the next one.

unlockPrimerCards.title: What a loop is
unlockPrimerCards.body: A loop repeats the code inside it, so one tactic can run several times.

unlockPrimerCards.title: Automation value
unlockPrimerCards.body: One loop can handle several preview balls in a row.

unlockPrimerCards.title: Skip safely
unlockPrimerCards.body: If you use 'skip_ball()', that branch must end before the same loop step reaches another launch. 'continue' works well for that.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 1: Repeat the helper at least three times
practiceGoals.instruction: Use 'for ... in range(3)' in the main program and put 'follow_portal()' inside the loop body so the helper launches at least three balls in one run.
practiceGoals.boardHint:
Write 'for shot in range(3):' and indent 'follow_portal()' underneath it.

The indented line runs three times, so the helper launches at least three balls.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 2: Repeat the helper at least four times
practiceGoals.instruction:
Auto-run is now available beside 'Run program'. It repeats runs for you.

Increase the loop count so the helper launches at least four balls in one run.
practiceGoals.boardHint: The previous goal used 'range(3)'. Change it to 'range(4)' for four launches.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 3: Handle every possible ball with a loop
practiceGoals.instruction:
Handle the 3 preview balls inside one loop.
On each repetition, check 'next_ball':
if it is 'portal_ball', call 'follow_portal()'
if it is 'negative_ball', skip it
if it is 'center_ball', send it to input 2.
practiceGoals.boardHint:
Put one 'if / elif / else' chain inside the loop.

In the negative-ball branch, use 'skip_ball()' and 'continue' so the same loop step does not accidentally launch. In the center-ball branch, choose input 2. Every remaining ball can go through the portal helper.

title: Lists
boardSubtitle: Read the 3 input multipliers, find the strongest one, and farm that input.
unlockSpotlightText:
Each input now has its own multiplier.
The board and Board data panel show the three values in 'bonus_map'.

'bonus_map[0]' matches input 1
'bonus_map[1]' matches input 2
'bonus_map[2]' matches input 3

The values change every run. Find the highest multiplier and farm that input.

unlockPrimerCards.title: What the list stores
unlockPrimerCards.body: 'bonus_map' stores the three input multipliers in order.

unlockPrimerCards.title: How indexes match inputs
unlockPrimerCards.body: 'bonus_map[0]' matches input 1, 'bonus_map[1]' matches input 2, and 'bonus_map[2]' matches input 3.

unlockPrimerCards.title: How to search the map
unlockPrimerCards.body: With a loop you can go through 'bonus_map', inspect its values, and remember which input has the highest multiplier.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 1: Compare two multipliers
practiceGoals.instruction:
Compare the multipliers in 'bonus_map[0]' and 'bonus_map[2]'.
Store the index of the higher multiplier in the variable 'best_index' and then launch one ball from the matching input.
For the comparison you can use 'if bonus_map[0] < bonus_map[2]:'
practiceGoals.boardHint:
Start by assuming the best index is 0. Then check whether 'bonus_map[2]' is greater than 'bonus_map[0]'. If it is, set 'best_index = 2'.

'choose_input(...)' needs an input number from 1-3, so use 'best_index + 1' at the end.
practiceGoals.suggestedSnippet:
best_index = 0
if bonus_map[2] > bonus_map[0]:
    best_index = 2
choose_input(best_index + 1)
drop_ball()

practiceGoals.title: Goal 2: Find the best multiplier out of all 3
practiceGoals.instruction: Turn the two-slot comparison into a full search. Use a 'for' loop to check all 3 multipliers, keep the highest value and its index, then launch one ball from that input.
practiceGoals.boardHint:
Loop over all of 'bonus_map'. Track the best multiplier so far, the index of that slot, and the current index.

When 'multiplier > best_multiplier', update both 'best_multiplier' and 'best_index'.
practiceGoals.suggestedSnippet:
best_multiplier = bonus_map[0]
best_index = 0
index = 0
for multiplier in bonus_map:
    if multiplier > best_multiplier:
        best_multiplier = multiplier
        best_index = index
    index = index + 1
choose_input(best_index + 1)
drop_ball()
practiceGoals.starterProgramSource:
best_index = 0
if bonus_map[2] > bonus_map[0]:
    best_index = 2
choose_input(best_index + 1)
drop_ball()

practiceGoals.title: Goal 3: Use the best input
practiceGoals.instruction: Use the same best-input search and then drop at least 3 balls from that input with a loop.
practiceGoals.boardHint:
The first part finds the best index just like the previous goal. After the search, add a second loop.

Inside the second loop, call 'choose_input(best_index + 1)' and 'drop_ball()' each time so the same best input runs at least three times.
practiceGoals.suggestedSnippet:
best_multiplier = bonus_map[0]
best_index = 0
index = 0
for multiplier in bonus_map:
    if multiplier > best_multiplier:
        best_multiplier = multiplier
        best_index = index
    index = index + 1
for shot in range(3):
    choose_input(best_index + 1)
    drop_ball()
practiceGoals.starterProgramSource:
best_multiplier = bonus_map[0]
best_index = 0
index = 0
for multiplier in bonus_map:
    if multiplier > best_multiplier:
        best_multiplier = multiplier
        best_index = index
    index = index + 1
choose_input(best_index + 1)
drop_ball()
